Back
January 2026·10 min read·Developer Tools

Rethinking Developer Experience

Developer experience has become a product category. But most tools that claim to improve DX are solving the wrong problem — they're reducing keystrokes when they should be reducing cognitive load.

These are not the same thing. A tool that auto-completes boilerplate still requires you to hold the boilerplate's structure in your head. A tool that eliminates the need to think about the boilerplate at all is genuinely different.

Cognitive Load as the Real Metric

The limiting factor in software development isn't typing speed or even syntax memorization. It's the number of things a developer needs to hold in working memory simultaneously to make forward progress.

This reframing changes how you design developer tools. Instead of asking "how do we make this faster?" ask "how do we make this require less mental state?"

Progressive Disclosure in Tooling

The best developer tools apply the principle of progressive disclosure: show only what's necessary for the current task, but make everything else accessible when needed. The zero-config default gets you started; the escape hatches let you go deep.

This is hard to build. It requires a sophisticated model of the user's mental state at each step, and deep investment in the defaults. But it's the difference between tools people tolerate and tools people love.

What We've Learned Building Canvas

In building Canvas, we've run into every version of this problem. The primitives that feel natural to systems engineers look alien to software developers. The abstractions that simplify common cases obscure uncommon ones. There's no clean answer — only continuous iteration driven by watching real users work.
